Cover Story

Meet Pseudomonas aeruginosa — My Tiny, Tenacious Companion

It doesn't cuddle. It doesn't fetch. But Pseudomonas aeruginosa has spent this semester surviving everything I threw at it — darkness, cold, and even a potato. Here's its story.

By Sungkar  ·  Microbiology Lab, Spring 2026
P. aeruginosa streak plate — the original culture
Introduction Background

What Is This Creature?

Pseudomonas aeruginosa is a gram-negative, rod-shaped bacterium found in soil, water, and wherever life dares to be interesting. It is an obligate aerobe — meaning it needs oxygen — and grows in the classic white-to-cream colonies you see on nutrient agar.

Visually, the colonies are circular, slightly raised, and sometimes produce a faint greenish pigment (pyoverdin) under UV light. On standard nutrient agar, growth appears as dense, opaque white streaks.

"If bacteria had a LinkedIn, P. aeruginosa would have a hundred skills endorsed."

It is also a well-studied opportunistic pathogen — capable of infecting plants and animals — making it scientifically fascinating. My pet is small, but it's not simple.

How I Kept It Alive All Semester

The culture was maintained on nutrient agar streak plates, incubated at room temperature (~25°C) and re-streaked weekly to keep colonies fresh and uncontaminated.

Each plate was sealed with Parafilm and labelled with the date and conditions. Plates were stored in the lab incubator between sessions. No special nutrients were needed — P. aeruginosa is remarkably unfussy about its diet.

Over the course of the semester, three experiments were designed to probe the bacterium's limits: its tolerance to light, its temperature preferences, and whether it could colonize potato tissue.

"What happens when you take an incredibly adaptable organism and test exactly how adaptable it really is?"

Quick Stats — P. aeruginosa

Cell typeGram-negative rod
OxygenObligate aerobe
Colony colorWhite / cream
Optimal temp~30–37°C
PigmentPyoverdin (yellow-green)
HabitatSoil, water, lab benches
Threat levelOpportunistic pathogen
Pet score10/10 ★★★★★
Experiment 1 Light vs. Dark
01

Does My Microbe Know What Day It Is?

Testing whether light exposure affects colony growth

Hypothesis

If P. aeruginosa is sensitive to UV light, then plates incubated under light conditions will show reduced or altered colony growth compared to dark-incubated plates.

Experimental Design

Four plates were prepared with streak inoculations of P. aeruginosa from the stock culture. Two plates were labelled "PA Control Light" and incubated in normal ambient lab light. Two plates were labelled "PA Control Dark / Experimental Dark" and wrapped in foil or placed in a dark environment for the same incubation period.

All plates used the same nutrient agar medium, the same source culture, and were incubated at the same temperature (~25°C) for 48–72 hours. Light exposure was the sole independent variable.

Results

Both light and dark plates showed robust colony growth. The white streak colonies appeared in similar density and morphology across conditions. No obvious inhibition of growth was observed under light, nor any visible difference in colony size, shape, or color.

Conclusion

Hypothesis: Not Supported

Light exposure did not detectably alter the growth of P. aeruginosa under these conditions. As a non-photosynthetic, soil-dwelling bacterium, this is unsurprising — P. aeruginosa does not rely on light for energy and is generally not sensitive to visible light at standard lab intensities. If UV light had been used, results might differ.

PA — Dark condition (experimental)
Light vs. Dark — side by side
Exp. Dark — 05.12.2026
Experiment 2 Temperature Tolerance
02

How Hot Is Too Hot?
(And How Cold Is Too Cold?)

Growth across a temperature gradient: 16°C, 20°C, 30°C, and 37°C

Hypothesis

If P. aeruginosa has an optimal growth temperature near mammalian body temperature, then plates incubated at 30–37°C will show greater colony growth than those at 16°C or 20°C.

Experimental Design

Eight streak plates were inoculated with the same stock culture and divided into four temperature groups (two replicate plates per temperature): 16°C, 20°C, 30°C, and 37°C. All plates used identical nutrient agar and the same inoculation technique.

Plates were placed in incubators set to the target temperatures for 48 hours. Colony density and morphology were compared visually after the incubation period. Temperature was the sole independent variable.

Results

Plates incubated at 30°C and 37°C showed dense, well-defined streak growth, consistent with healthy colony proliferation. At 20°C, growth was visible but appeared thinner. The 16°C plates showed the least visible growth, with sparse or faint colony streaks.

All temperatures permitted some growth, confirming P. aeruginosa's broad thermal tolerance — but growth was clearly more vigorous at warmer temperatures.

Conclusion

Hypothesis: Supported

Results are consistent with P. aeruginosa's known optimal growth range of 30–37°C. The bacterium grew at all tested temperatures, confirming its psychrotolerant nature (it can survive at 4°C), but peak growth occurred near body temperature — a trait that contributes to its clinical relevance.

All 4 temperature conditions (04.09.2026)
T 16°C, 20°C, 30°C, 37°C — replicate plates
Experiment 3 Pathogenicity Test
03

Can My Microbe Ruin a Potato?

Splash inoculation on potato slices — testing plant pathogenicity

Hypothesis

If P. aeruginosa can colonize plant tissue, then inoculated potato slices will show greater browning, softening, or surface growth compared to uninoculated controls after 48–72 hours.

Experimental Design

Potato slices were cut to similar sizes and placed on filter paper in petri dishes to provide a moist environment. Two dishes received a "splash inoculation" — a small volume of P. aeruginosa liquid culture applied directly to the surface. One dish served as the uninoculated control.

All dishes were sealed and incubated at room temperature for 48–72 hours. Changes in tissue color, texture, and any visible colony growth on the potato surface were recorded as results.

Results

The inoculated potato slices showed notable browning and surface discoloration compared to the control. The splash-inoculated slices appeared to have a slightly softened texture at the inoculation site, with a darker, more irregular surface appearance. The control potato slice remained firmer and lighter in color.

No macroscopic surface colonies were clearly visible, but the discoloration pattern on inoculated slices was distinct from oxidation-only browning expected in the control.

Conclusion

Hypothesis: Partially Supported

The inoculated potato slices showed more browning than the control, suggesting that P. aeruginosa may have affected the tissue — potentially through the secretion of extracellular enzymes (such as proteases or lipases) that degrade plant cell walls. However, since potato slices naturally brown due to oxidation, a longer incubation period or a quantitative measure (e.g., pH change, cell maceration area) would strengthen the conclusion. The result is suggestive, not definitive.

Control vs. splash inoculation (day 3)
Inoculated slices — visible browning
Control — Sungkar potato
Final Thoughts Conclusions & Reflections

What I Learned from Living With a Bacterium

Pseudomonas aeruginosa turned out to be exactly as advertised: stubborn, adaptable, and surprisingly hard to impress. It grew in the light. It grew in the dark. It thrived at body temperature and held its own even in the cold.

The temperature experiment was the most revealing. Seeing clear differences in colony density across a 21°C range reinforced that even without obvious "preferences," organisms have evolved optimal zones — and that those zones aren't arbitrary. P. aeruginosa's affinity for 37°C explains its clinical significance.

The potato experiment raised more questions than it answered, which is perhaps the most honest outcome of any experiment. The browning differential was real, but controlling for oxidation would require a more rigorous design — perhaps comparing with a heat-killed bacterial control or measuring pH at the inoculation site. That's worth doing.

The light experiment, though its result was "nothing happened," was still valuable. A null result is a result. Confirming that this non-photosynthetic, soil-adapted organism doesn't care about visible light is consistent with what we know about its ecology.

Most unexpectedly: I developed an appreciation for how much biology happens at scales you can barely see. Every plate I streaked, every colony I counted, every label I wrote upside down — it was contact with something alive and indifferent and endlessly interesting.

What's Next for My Pet?

Test antibiotic sensitivity — which antibiotics actually stop it, and at what concentration?
Biofilm formation assay — P. aeruginosa is a notorious biofilm former; can we see it under a microscope?
UV light experiment — repeat Exp. 1 with actual UV radiation rather than visible light.
Quantify the potato result — measure maceration zone area or use pH paper to control for simple oxidation browning.
Pigment induction — under iron-limiting conditions, does pyoverdin (the fluorescent pigment) actually appear on plates?
Final Observation
"It's not the biggest. It's not the flashiest. But it showed up — every single time."
